ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to egg mayonnaise sandwich; mapped to broader sandwich food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers sandwiches as a category; no specific egg mayo sandwich code exists. Broader category used as defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for egg mayo sandwich; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 sandwich (approximately 2 slices white bread with egg mayo filling)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one sandwich made with two slices of white bread and an egg mayonnaise filling, weighing approximately 120 g.

nutrient_source.title: Egg Mayo Sandwich — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Egg Mayo Sandwich (cr_id F05020039). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — white bread GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for egg mayo sandwich as a composite dish. GI estimated at 70 based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle: white bread, which has a well-established GI of approximately 70 (glucose reference) per the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI table. The egg and mayonnaise components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI of the sandwich. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (70) × available carbohydrate per serving (24.25 g/100g × 120 g serving = 29.1 g) / 100 = 20.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 339 mg/100g with very low potassium offset (57 mg/100g) and low dietary fibre (1.09 g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and consider switching to wholemeal bread and reduced-sodium mayonnaise, as the sodium-to-potassium ratio is unfavourable and dietary fibre is insufficient to provide a meaningful cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white bread base (GI ~70) with 24.25 g/100g refined carbohydrate and only 1.09 g/100g dietary fibre, promoting rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this sandwich in its white-bread form; substituting wholemeal or multigrain bread and pairing with a high-fibre side dish will substantially reduce the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 166.37 mg/100g from egg yolk and sodium 339 mg/100g from bread and mayonnaise, with low dietary fibre and unknown saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption due to the elevated cholesterol and sodium content; choosing wholemeal bread, reducing mayonnaise quantity, and increasing vegetable content would improve the cardiovascular profile of this meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg Mayo Sandwich is a classic Western-style sandwich consisting of two slices of white bread filled with a mixture of hard-boiled egg and mayonnaise. It is a popular ready-to-eat or home-prepared meal widely consumed in Singapore as a breakfast item and school canteen staple. The filling is creamy and mildly savoury, with a soft texture from the chopped or mashed egg combined with the richness of mayonnaise. The white bread provides a soft, slightly chewy base. Per 100 g, the sandwich delivers 227 kcal, 9.77 g protein, 10.14 g fat, 24.25 g carbohydrate, 2.36 g sugar, 1.09 g dietary fibre, and 339 mg sodium. Cholesterol is notably elevated at 166.37 mg/100g due to the egg yolk content. The glycaemic index is approximately 70 (high), driven by the refined white bread base, making this sandwich a concern for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium is in the borderline-high range with very low potassium (57 mg/100g), which is unfavourable for blood pressure management. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains gluten and egg allergens. It is not suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes without modification, and should be consumed in moderation by those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: How many calories in an egg mayo sandwich?
Is egg mayo sandwich good for diabetics?
Can I eat egg mayo sandwich if I have high blood pressure?
Is egg mayo sandwich high in cholesterol?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020039) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match to the canonical HPB record for Egg Mayo Sandwich. GI is an analogous estimate based on the white bread component using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this composite egg mayo sandwich are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F05020039) as of 2026. No supplementary breakdown was available from USDA FoodData Central for this exact composite preparation. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white bread (the dominant carbohydrate vehicle) per the University of Sydney GI Database; the egg and mayonnaise components contribute negligible carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (70) × carbohydrate per serving (29.1 g) / 100 = 20.4, using a 120 g serving size. Halal status assumed based on standard ingredients; kosher status not confirmed. Saturated fat data unavailable from HPB source — this is a limitation for cardiovascular disease assessment.
